data.title: Gold price turns lower as dollar bounces higher, but debt concerns linger
data.description:
Gold futures turned lower Thursday after a three-day winning streak, as the dollar strengthened and investors locked in profits after prices touched a two-week high earlier in the session.

The U.S. dollar index rose 0.5%, rebounding after a steep slide since Moody's started the week by downgrading its U.S. credit rating, which exacerbated concerns over the U.S. debt burden and fiscal outlook, weighing on risk sentiment in recent days; a higher dollar makes bullion more expensive for foreign currency holders.

The U.S. bond market has been concerned about President Trump's "big beautiful" tax and spending bill, which finally passed the House of Representatives by a single vote and now moves on to the Senate; the legislation, as currently written, would add ~$3.8T to the federal government's $36.2B in debt over the next decade, according to the Congressional Budget Office, but includes an increase in the debt limit.

"We're seeing some profit taking pressure from recent gains and a firmer U.S. dollar index is another bearish factor," Kitco Metals analyst Jim Wycoff said in a note, but "the specter of a shaky global bond market is going to be a bullish underlying factor for the gold market that's going to limit the downside."

Meanwhile, traders continue to monitor developments in the Middle East, with another round of nuclear talks between the U.S. and Iran set to take place on Friday.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for May delivery ended -0.5% to $3,292.30/oz, and front-month May Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished -1.2% to $33.047/oz, snapping three-session winning streaks for both precious metals.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Ross Stores withdraws annual guidance amid tariff uncertainty, projects Q2 EPS range of $1.40–$1.55
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ross Stores (ROST) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Conroy emphasized the company’s resilience despite a volatile external environment, stating, "total sales grew 3% to $5 billion with comparable store sales flat versus last year. Earnings per share were $1.47 compared to $1.46 last year, while net income for the period was $479 million versus $488 million for the same period in 2024."
  • Conroy highlighted sequential monthly sales improvement, with cosmetics as the strongest merchandise area and the Southeast leading geographically. He noted, "our dd's DISCOUNTS brand continued its strong momentum from 2024 with another quarter of solid sales and operating profits."
  • Conroy addressed tariff impacts, saying the company will work to balance pricing and merchandise margin compression, adding, "it is possible that we will see short-term pressure on our profitability."
  • Inventory strategy was detailed with "total consolidated inventories were up 88% versus last year mainly due to opportunistic buys during the period. Average store inventories were up 4% in line with our plan and packaway merchandise represented 41% of total inventory."
  • Conroy announced the opening of 16 new Ross and 3 dd’s DISCOUNTS locations, continuing the plan for about 90 new stores this year, and stated, "there are simply too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility into the second half of the fiscal year and we believe it is prudent to withdraw our previously provided annual guidance at this time."
  • CFO Adam Orvos reported, "first quarter operating margin of 12.2% was similar to last year. Cost of goods sold was relatively unchanged from a year ago. Merchandise margin declined 45 basis points, mainly due to higher ocean freight costs and the initial impact of tariffs."
  • Orvos added, "during the first quarter, we repurchased 2 million shares of common stock for an aggregate cost of $263 million under the company's two-year $2.1 billion authorization."

Outlook

  • Orvos provided Q2 guidance: "comparable store sales are projected to be flat to up 3%. Earnings per share for the second quarter are now projected to be in the range of $1.40 to $1.55 and includes a cost impact of $0.11 to $0.16 from the announced tariffs."
  • Total sales for Q2 are forecast to increase 2% to 6% versus the prior year, with operating margin projected between 10.7% and 11.4%, including a 90 to 120 basis point negative tariff impact.
  • Management withdrew full-year guidance, citing "too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility into the second half of the fiscal year."

Financial Results

  • Sales and earnings performed at the high-end of company expectations for the quarter. Operating margin was flat at 12.2% year-over-year.
  • Merchandise margin was pressured by tariffs and ocean freight; occupancy and distribution costs rose modestly.
  • SG&A was flat year-over-year, as lower incentive compensation was offset by sales deleverage.
  • Inventory was notably higher due to opportunistic buying, with packaway merchandise steady as a share of inventory.
  • Share buyback activity continued, and the company remains on track for a $1.05 billion repurchase in 2025.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about drivers of sequential comp improvement and May trends. Conroy responded, "The sequential improvement was really broad-based across the merchandise hierarchy... we did guide to a flat to a plus three. So that should give you some sense of how we feel about the health of the business." Hartshorn explained tariff mitigation strategies, including vendor negotiations, careful pricing, and leveraging closeouts and packaway inventory.
  • Lorraine Hutchinson, Bank of America: Inquired about the gross margin hit from tariffs and whether impact may decrease. Hartshorn said, "Second quarter impact really includes... orders that were already in place when the tariffs were announced... we'll have to wait and see how the macro economy and retail environment evolves and what the outlook looks for inflation."
  • Mark Altschwager, Baird: Asked about inventory risk and strategic initiatives. Conroy noted, "in the short-term, I think there'll be availability of closeouts... we think we can get through that with no problem."
  • Tracy Kogan, Citigroup: Queried about tariff mitigation and customer trade down. Hartshorn said, "In the second quarter, it does include some mitigation," and saw broad-based comps across income bands.
  • Michael Binetti, Evercore ISI: Requested guidance range rationale and sourcing flexibility. Hartshorn explained the broader range was "out of abundance of caution given the macroeconomic and geopolitical environments."
  • Alexandra Straton, Morgan Stanley: Asked about branded strategy and women's apparel. Conroy stated, "we feel very good about the team's execution of the branded strategy... we don't expect margin headwinds going forward any longer from the brand strategy." Ladies business was slightly better than chain average.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on tariff impacts, inventory management, and the company’s visibility, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone, pressing for specifics on mitigation and strategic flexibility.
  • Management maintained a cautious, pragmatic tone, often reiterating uncertainty and highlighting multiple mitigation levers. Conroy stated, "there are simply too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility."
  • Compared to last quarter, the tone was more defensive, with management openly acknowledging lower visibility and the withdrawal of annual guidance as a prudent step.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a previously provided annual outlook in Q4 to a withdrawal in Q1, signaling increased uncertainty due to tariffs and macroeconomic factors.
  • Management expanded discussion of tariff impacts and mitigation, which was only a minor topic last quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from branded strategy and store productivity in Q4 to tariff risk, inventory strategy, and the flexibility of sourcing in Q1.
  • Management’s confidence in key initiatives, such as the branded strategy, remained, but overall tone reflected greater caution and less optimism regarding near-term trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated tariffs on Chinese imports, with management warning of "short-term pressure on our profitability."
  • Limited visibility on second-half performance due to consumer sentiment, inflation, and trade policy unpredictability.
  • Possible receipt risk from supply chain disruptions, though inventory is positioned to mitigate gaps.
  • Management cited broad-based inflationary pressure and acknowledged the potential for further impacts if macro conditions worsen.

Final Takeaway

Ross Stores delivered Q1 results at the high end of expectations despite a challenging environment, with improved sales momentum, solid inventory positioning, and strong execution of strategic initiatives such as the branded strategy. However, elevated tariff risk and macro uncertainty prompted the withdrawal of annual guidance, with management focusing on multiple mitigation levers and maintaining a cautious approach for the coming quarters. The outlook for Q2 projects comparable sales between flat and up 3%, and EPS in the $1.40 to $1.55 range, reflecting ongoing tariff impacts and a measured stance amid unpredictable trade and consumer conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump threatens to limit imports if countries don't pay more for drugs
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday threatened to limit imports of products from foreign countries if they don't work with pharmaceutical companies to pay more for drugs as a way to lower the costs for medicines in the United States.

Earlier this month, the president signed an Executive Order tying the prices of prescription drugs in the U.S. to what they cost in other countries under a program dubbed "Most Favored Nation."

If countries fight against drugmakers, "That's ok. We are not going to let you send any more cars into the United States," Trump said at a White House event for the release of the Make America Healthy Again report.

"Or we're not going to let you sell more wine or liquor or alcohol, or something that's actually much more important to them than the drugs. And we're going to be able to force that issue if we need to."

In his remarks, Trump predicted that Americans would save as much as 80% on prescription drugs compared to what they pay now with the Most Favored Nation program.

He also contended the MFN wouldn't impact pharmaceutical companies' bottom lines. "There shouldn't be a hit on their stock," adding that their income source will be "redistributed...so [other countries are] going to pay a little more, and we're going to pay a lot less."

data.title: MAHA report: 'Overmedicalization, poor diet' factors for childhood chronic diseases
data.description:
An assessment from the Make America Healthy Again Commission cites "overmedicalization, poor diet, aggregation of environmental chemicals, and lack of physical activity and chronic stress" as key reasons for the "childhood chronic disease crisis."

The report, mandated by President Trump through an Executive Action creating the panel, deals specifically with "the stark reality of children's declining health." It is titled "Make Our Children Healthy Again: Assessment."

HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy chairs the commission with Vincent Haley, Assistant to the President for Domestic Policy, as executive director.

"There is a concerning trend of overprescribing medications to
children, often driven by conflicts of interest in medical research, regulation, and practice," the assessment states regarding drugs. "This has led to unnecessary treatments and long-term health risks."

The report also targets ultra-processed foods, noting that about 70% of children's calories come from them, leading to obesity, diabetes, and other conditions. 

It also expresses concerns about children's exposure to synthetic chemicals, "some of which have been linked to developmental issues
and chronic disease."

High levels of inactivity, smartphone use, sleep deprivation, and chronic stress are contributing "to the rise in chronic diseases and mental health
challenges."

The document also targets childhood vaccine schedules, noting that the schedule in the U.S. has more shots than many European nations. "Despite the growth of the childhood vaccine schedule, there has been limited scientific inquiry into the links between vaccines and chronic disease, the impacts of vaccine injury, and conflicts of interest in the development of the vaccine schedule. These areas warrant future inquiry."

On Wednesday, The Wall Street Journal published an article detailing parts of the assessment.

By mid-August, the MAHA Commission is required to publish as strategy to address its findings.

data.title: Inomin Mines announces non-brokered private placement
data.description:
• Inomin Mines (TSXV:MINE:CA) announced on Thursday a non-brokered private placement of securities to raise total gross proceeds up to $200,000
  • The financing will consist of non-flow-through units (the "NFT Units") to be sold at a price of $0.035 per NFT Unit, said the company. 
  • "Each NFT unit will consist of one common share of the company and one common share purchase warrant. Each Warrant will entitle the holder thereof to purchase one additional share for a period of two years from the date of issuance at an exercise price of $0.05 per share," stated the company, adding that,  "a maximum of 10 million NFT units will be issued in the financing."
  • Inomin intends to allocate the gross proceeds raised from the sale of the NFT Units as follows: 50% for general working capital; 35% to pay non-arm's length management and geological service fees; 10% for investor relations activities; and 5% for property evaluations.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Supreme Court preserves Fed independence, lets Trump shake up other agencies
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court on Thursday blocked President Donald Trump from removing Federal Reserve officials but cleared the way for firings at other independent federal agencies, according to a filing.

The Fed is a  "uniquely structured, quasi-private entity" that follows in the distinct historical tradition of the First and Second Banks of the United States," the court said in the Trump v. Wilcox document.

The decision comes as Trump has previously brought up the possibility of dismissing Fed Chair Jerome Powell, an unfavorable suggestion among market participants.

But Trump still got the green light to fire officials at other independent federal agencies. 

The high court's liberal justices opposed the ruling that permitted the Trump layoffs.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Goodyear Tire & Rubber sells majority of chemical business
data.description:
• The Goodyear Tire & Rubber (NASDAQ:GT) has sold a majority of its chemical business to an affiliate of private equity firm, Gemspring Capital Management, for $650M.
  • The acquisition includes two facilities in Houston and Beaumont, Texas, and an R&D facility in Akron, Ohio.
  • Goodyear (NASDAQ:GT) will retail its chemical facilities in Niagara Falls, NY and Bayport, Texas and rights to all products produced at these facilities.
  • The transaction is expected to close in late 2025.
  • The transaction is in line with the company’s Goodyear Forward program to achieve record quarterly savings and at least $2B in asset sales.
  • “With the sale of our chemical business, we continue to demonstrate our commitment to optimizing our portfolio and creating shareholder value,” said Goodyear CEO Mark Stewart.
  • SEC filing
